title: No Small Danger
subtitle: Aurelia Techosis, Daughter of Diodorus -- The Small Oasis to Oxyrhynchus, 295 CE
summary: While a woman buries her mother, her neighbors walk in and take everything — gold, furniture, clothing. She travels from the Western Desert to Oxyrhynchus to petition a Roman officer, and her illiterate voice, dictated to a scribe, survives on the back of someone else's petition.

sections.title: The Petition
sections.body_html:
The man she petitioned was Aurelius Ammonius, benefikiarios eparchou Aigyptou – beneficiarius of the praefect of Egypt.7 He was a soldier, stationed at a fixed post in Oxyrhynchus, whose job was to receive complaints, issue orders, and forward serious cases to the governor’s office in Alexandria.8 He was, in the language of modern policing, something between a desk sergeant and a local prosecutor. In the language of Roman administration, he was the praefect’s eyes and ears in the countryside, the point where a woman from the desert could make contact with the vast machinery of imperial justice.

Techosis did not write the petition herself. A man named Aurelius Agathodaemon, son of Serenus, held the pen.9 The document concludes with the standard formula: “written for her by Aurelius Agathodaemon son of Serenus, as she stated that she did not know letters” – me eidenai grammata.10 This was not unusual. Across the hundreds of petitions that survive from Roman Egypt, illiterate women appear again and again, speaking their cases aloud while scribes rendered their words into the formal Greek that the bureaucracy required.11 The scribe’s hand is visible; the voice is hers.

And it is a carefully constructed voice. The petition opens not with the facts but with a warning:

“No small danger and no ordinary severity awaits those who lightly give themselves over to plunder and robbery of the property of others.”12

Kindynos ou mikros. No small danger. The opening is not addressed to Sotas and Papontos, who will presumably never read it. It is addressed to the beneficiarius – a reminder, couched in the passive voice and the conditional mood, that the law takes a dim view of robbery and that the official who receives this petition has an obligation to act. It is a legal commonplace, a rhetorical flourish meant to signal that the petitioner knows what she is doing – that she understands the consequences the law prescribes for theft, and that she expects those consequences to be enforced.13 It is a threat wrapped in deference, the kind of opening that a scribe familiar with petition formulae would know how to deploy. Whether the words originated with Techosis or with Agathodaemon is impossible to say. Perhaps she told him what had happened, and he shaped it into the language the beneficiarius expected to read. Perhaps she was more specific than we can know. The petition, in any case, speaks in her name and for her interests, and its logic is sharp.

After the warning, the narrative. Her mother fell ill. Techosis nursed her – “in the goodness of my heart,” she says, though the Greek carries a stronger sense of moral obligation: ta preponta geinesthai hypo teknon goneousi, “what is fitting to be done by children for parents.”14 The phrase is worth dwelling on. It does not say “because I loved her,” though perhaps she did. It says “what is fitting” – what is owed, what is expected, what the social order demands. The word preponta connotes propriety, decorum, the things that must be done. Techosis is not appealing to her emotions. She is appealing to a norm.

This was not merely sentiment. In both Egyptian and Roman legal culture, the filial duty of care – what the Greeks called geroboskia, the nourishing of the aged – carried weight in inheritance disputes.15 Tax registers from Roman Egypt actually categorized certain individuals as “men chosen by the parents from their sons to support them in old age” – a legal designation with fiscal consequences.80 By establishing that she had fulfilled her obligations as a daughter, Techosis was building her case as heir before she ever mentioned the robbery.

Her mother died. A few days passed – the petition says “a few days ago,” pro oligon hemeron, giving us the narrow temporal window between death and filing.16 Techosis arranged the funeral and “did all that was fitting on the occasion of her death” – ta kathekonta epi to thanato exetelesa. The word kathekonta echoes preponta – again the language of duty, of what must be done, of proper form. Then she discovered what Sotas and Papontos had done.

sections.title: The Mother's Death
sections.body_html:
The mother, also named Techosis, died intestate – adiathetou, without a will.17 The document does not say what killed her, only that she “was stricken with illness.” It does not say how long the illness lasted – days, weeks, months. It does not say whether a physician attended her, though Oxyrhynchus had public physicians who were expected to examine the sick and the dead on request.81 It does not say whether anyone else visited, or whether the daughter nursed her alone in a room that smelled of sickness while the neighbors went about their lives on the other side of the wall.

She died in Oxyrhynchus, in the house she shared with those neighbors. She may have been old or she may not have been; illness in late-third-century Egypt did not require old age to be fatal. What we know is that her daughter was present, that her daughter nursed her, and that no one else seems to have been there to help.

There is no mention of a husband. No brothers are named. No other heirs are suggested. No father appears – Diodorus, whose Greek name graces the address formula, is nowhere present in the narrative of illness and death and theft. He may have died years earlier. He may have remained in the Small Oasis when his wife and daughter moved to Oxyrhynchus. He may have been alive somewhere else entirely, irrelevant to a case between women and their neighbors. The petition does not explain his absence because the petition does not need to. Techosis is the one who nursed the dying woman. Techosis is the one who paid for the funeral. Techosis is the one who came back to find the rooms emptied.

The legal phrase she deploys – kleronomo kata tous nomous, “heir in accordance with the laws” – invokes the Roman rules of intestate succession that, after the Constitutio Antoniniana of 212 CE, applied to all citizens in Egypt.18 Under those rules, children were primary heirs. Daughters inherited equally with sons.19 Techosis’s claim was straightforward: her mother died without a will, she was the only child present (perhaps the only child at all), and the law made her the heir.

The Gnomon of the Idios Logos, the great administrative handbook that governed fiscal matters in Roman Egypt, stated the alternative plainly: “Of those who die intestate and have no other heir according to the laws, their property is adjudicated to the fiscus.”20 The state took what no family member claimed. This was not an abstract threat. Roman Egypt was a province where the imperial treasury pursued unclaimed estates with the same diligence it pursued unpaid taxes. Officials called the idios logos – literally “private account” – were specifically tasked with identifying and confiscating the property of those who died without heirs.

Techosis was not going to let the state take her mother’s property. But she was also not going to let Sotas and Papontos take it first. She was fighting on two fronts, though the petition addresses only one of them explicitly.

The funeral itself would have required expenditure. Linen for wrapping – and Techosis would have known, with a specificity that does not appear in the petition, whether the linen was good or poor, whether the wrapping was generous or thin. Payments to the nekrotaphoi, the undertakers who handled the dead.21 Oil for anointing. Offerings, perhaps, at the local temple – to which gods we do not know, whether the old Egyptian ones or the newer Greek ones or some syncretic fusion that the inhabitants of third-century Oxyrhynchus would not have thought to distinguish. Incense, perhaps. Professional mourners, perhaps. The rituals of death in Roman Egypt were layered with tradition and obligation, and they were not cheap.

Techosis bore these costs, and she says so in the petition – not out of grief alone, but because paying for the funeral was further proof that she was acting as heir. She was performing the role, publicly and at her own expense. Every drachma she spent on her mother’s death was an argument for her right to her mother’s property.

And it was while she was doing all of this – occupied with the logistics of death, with the rituals and the payments and the crushing administrative burden of dying in the Roman Empire – that her neighbors struck.

sections.title: What They Stole
sections.body_html:
The petition lists what Sotas and Papontos carried off in broad categories rather than specific inventories. This is both a legal convention and, perhaps, a sign of how much was taken. Techosis claims they seized:

“Extensive movables” – kinoumeois: the word covers anything that could be picked up and carried away, as opposed to the walls and floors and doorframes of the building itself.22

“A considerable amount of gold” – chryso ouk oligo: literally “gold not a little.” The phrase is deliberately vague. Techosis may not have known exactly how much gold her mother possessed; she may not have wanted to commit to a specific figure that could be challenged in court. But “not a small amount” suggests something more than a few coins. Jewelry, perhaps. Bracelets or earrings or the gold ornaments that Egyptian women of means wore and stored and passed to their daughters.23 Gold in Roman Egypt was weighed, assessed, and taxed. It was the most portable form of wealth – easy to carry, easy to hide, easy to deny you had ever possessed. If Sotas and Papontos had melted down whatever they took, the gold would have been untraceable. This may be why Techosis was in such a hurry to file the petition: not just to seek justice, but to create a record of what had existed before it could be transformed into something unrecognizable.

“A quantity of furniture” – endomeneia: household goods, the material infrastructure of daily life. Chairs, tables, beds, storage chests. A bed frame in Roman Egypt was not a thing you replaced easily. It was built by a carpenter, purchased or commissioned, carried into a room and used for decades. To lose your furniture was to lose the physical shape of your life – the chair you sat in, the table you ate at, the chest where you kept the gold and the clothing that were also now gone.24

“Very valuable clothes” – estheti polutimotate: the superlative is notable. Not merely good clothing but most valuable clothing. Textiles in Roman Egypt were significant stores of wealth. A fine linen garment could represent weeks of a weaver’s labor. Dyed fabrics, embroidered pieces, imported woolens – these were not just things to wear but things to sell, to pledge, to bequeath.25 Egyptian women accumulated textiles the way other people accumulated money, and for good reason: cloth held its value, could be stored indefinitely in the dry climate, and could be converted to cash at any market. When a man in mid-third-century Egypt complained that his departing wife had carried off “a large white undressed garment, an Oxyrhynchite pillow, a cross-striped double-blanket, materials for two tunics,” he was cataloguing real economic losses, not hurt feelings.26

And then: “and other things.” The inventory trails off into silence, the Greek equivalent of et cetera dropping like a curtain over whatever else the mother owned. Cooking vessels, lamps, tools, linens, stored grain, personal effects that carried sentimental rather than monetary value, the small accumulated objects of a life lived for decades – none of it is named. The scribe wrote “and other things,” and moved on. The brevity may reflect Techosis’s choice, Agathodaemon’s editorial judgment, or simply the convention of the form. The beneficiarius may not have needed an itemized inventory. The categories – gold, furniture, clothing – may have been enough. Or the list may simply have been too long for a petition already making a broader legal argument.

What the stolen goods tell us, collectively, is that the mother Techosis was not destitute. A woman who owned gold, fine clothing, and enough furniture to furnish a dwelling was a woman of some means. She was not wealthy by the standards of the Oxyrhynchite elite – no land is mentioned, no slaves, no claims to municipal office. But she was comfortable. She had accumulated property worth stealing, and worth fighting to recover.

sections.title: The Small Oasis
sections.body_html:
Techosis identifies herself in the petition’s address formula as being “of the Small Oasis, now living at the illustrious and most illustrious city of Oxyrhynchus” – Mikras Oaseos, katageinomenes en te lampra kai lamprotate Oxyryncheiton polei.27 The doubled honorific – “illustrious and most illustrious” – was standard titulature for Oxyrhynchus in this period, the kind of civic flattery that appeared on every official document. But the geographical contrast is striking. She came from the desert. She lived in the city. The petition records both facts because Roman law required it: your origo, your place of origin, followed you through life, even if you had long since left.28

The Small Oasis – Oasis parva in Latin, Mikra Oasis in Greek – is what we now call the Bahariya Oasis.29 It sits in a depression in the Western Desert about 370 kilometers from the Nile, surrounded by black basalt hills and iron-rich ridges, sustained by artesian springs that rise through the limestone and create pockets of green in an ocean of sand. The geographer Strabo called it “the Second Oasis.” The fifth-century historian Olympiodorus called it “the Third.” The Egyptians who lived there called it home.

In Techosis’s time, it was part of the Roman provincial administration, grouped with the Heptanomia – the seven nomes (administrative districts) of Middle Egypt – under the authority of the epistrategos, the regional governor who oversaw that cluster of districts.30 A proclamation by the praefect Valerius Pompeianus, issued just eight years before Techosis’s petition, was addressed jointly to the strategi of “the Heptanomia and the Small Oasis,” confirming that the oasis had its own strategus and its own administrative identity within the larger provincial structure.31

But the Small Oasis was also, crucially, linked to the Oxyrhynchite nome. Another proclamation by the same praefect Pompeianus, concerning the appointment of guardians for orphans, was addressed to the exegetes of “the Oxyrhynchite nome and the Small Oasis.”32 This administrative connection may explain why Techosis and her mother migrated to Oxyrhynchus rather than to some other Nile valley city. They went where the bureaucratic threads already ran. They went where the oasis was known.

Life in the Small Oasis was not primitive. Archaeological evidence reveals a Roman military fort at Qarat el-Toub, and papyri confirm that troops were garrisoned there, drawn from the larger military station at Oxyrhynchus.33 A document from 299 CE – just four years after Techosis’s petition – records an official named Aurelius Plutarchus being sent to “the Small Oasis for the discharge of the soldiers stationed there, in accordance with the order of my lord the most honourable praefect of Egypt.”34 Soldiers went back and forth between Oxyrhynchus and the oasis. Officials were dispatched. Orders were sent. The desert was not empty; it was administered.

There were springs, date palms, irrigated fields, temples, and the desert roads that connected the oasis to the Nile valley, monitored by Roman fortresses along the way.35 The Greco-Roman period was, by most archaeological measures, a time of prosperity for Bahariya – the springs were productive, the agriculture was viable, the connections to the Nile valley were maintained. Techosis grew up in this world, or was born into it, or arrived there as a child. She knew the desert. She knew what it meant to live far from the river.

But it was remote. The journey from Bahariya to the Nile took days, possibly a week, across open desert.36 People who made that journey – and the papyri show that they did, for trade, for litigation, for military service, for the simple pull of economic opportunity in a larger world – were leaving one kind of life for another.

We do not know why Techosis and her mother left. Economic hardship, perhaps. Family connections in Oxyrhynchus. A marriage that brought one of them to the city, followed by the other. A debt owed by someone in the valley to someone in the oasis – one earlier document records exactly such a cross-regional debt, with a creditor in the Nile valley attempting to collect from a debtor in the Small Oasis through the oasis’s strategus.75 The commercial links were real. People, goods, and obligations moved along the desert roads, and eventually people followed.

The papyrus does not say why they came. It records only the fact of displacement: a woman from the edge, now living in the center, still carrying the desert in her name.

What it must have meant to be “of the Small Oasis” in Oxyrhynchus – whether the designation carried stigma, or exoticism, or indifference, or the faint condescension of city dwellers toward people from the provinces – we can only guess. Oxyrhynchus was a cosmopolitan city, a place where Greek names and Egyptian names and Roman names mingled in the same family, where temples to Serapis stood alongside shrines to older gods, where the bureaucracy conducted its business in Greek but much of the population spoke Egyptian at home. A woman from Bahariya would not have been the strangest thing the city had seen. But she would have been marked, in legal documents at least, as someone from somewhere else. The origo followed you. It was the first thing the scribe wrote down, after your name.

sections.title: A Woman in Diocletian's Egypt
sections.body_html:
The year 295 CE fell in the first decade of Diocletian’s sweeping reorganization of the Roman Empire. The tetrarchic system was barely a decade old. Two Augusti – Diocletian in the East, Maximian in the West – and two Caesars – Constantius and Galerius – shared power in a structure designed to prevent the civil wars and usurpations that had torn the empire apart in the preceding half-century.37 Techosis’s petition is dated under all four rulers, as required: “In the consulship of the present consuls” followed by the regnal years of each tetrarch.38

In Egypt, the reforms were felt acutely. Diocletian would eventually split the old province into multiple smaller units, introduce a new tax system based on regular censuses, and – in 303 CE, eight years after Techosis filed her petition – launch the Great Persecution against Christians, the last and most violent attempt to suppress the new religion.39 The praefect who would carry out that persecution in Egypt, Clodius Culcianus, was not yet in office in 295. But the administrative machinery was already tightening. Census declarations, tax assessments, liturgical nominations, military requisitions – the bureaucratic weight of the late Roman state pressed down on every household.40

None of this appears in Techosis’s petition. She was not thinking about the Tetrarchy. She was thinking about her mother’s gold. But the Tetrarchy shaped the world in which she could think about it – the world in which a beneficiarius sat in a statio in Oxyrhynchus, in which petitions were dated by the regnal years of four simultaneous emperors, in which a woman from a desert oasis could invoke Roman law in a Greek-speaking city and expect someone to listen.

Oxyrhynchus in these years was a functioning city with a senate, a prytanis, gymnasiarchs, and a full complement of civic officers.41 Three years before Techosis’s petition, the city council had met to discuss sending a delegate to the praefect’s court.42 Village administrators in the surrounding countryside swore oaths by “the fortune of our lords Diocletian and Maximian” to guard the grain at the threshing floors until the tax collectors had taken their share.43 A youth was registered under the Diocletianic census in the same year.44 The Oxyrhynchus senate had recently appointed a new exegetes – the magistrate who oversaw guardianship cases and other legal-administrative business.78 The machinery of civic governance ground on, indifferent to who sat on the imperial throne, as long as someone did.

The world Techosis inhabited was one of forms and officers and obligations – a world in which even an illiterate woman from the desert knew to petition a beneficiarius, not a neighbor, not a village elder, but a representative of the praefect himself.

This was not naivety. It may have been strategic. Papyrological evidence suggests that provincials in Egypt sometimes preferred submitting petitions to military personnel like beneficiarii over local civilian magistrates, perceiving them as more impartial – as outsiders less likely to be entangled in local networks of patronage and obligation.45 Techosis, herself an outsider in Oxyrhynchus, may have calculated that a soldier answerable to the praefect would be a better ally than a city official who might know Sotas and Papontos personally.

Or perhaps it was simpler than that. Perhaps the beneficiarius’s statio was the closest office to her door. Perhaps Agathodaemon the scribe told her where to go. Perhaps someone in the neighborhood pointed her in the right direction – the way you might tell a stranger where the police station is. The petition does not record the decision-making process that led Techosis to Aurelius Ammonius’s desk. It records only that she arrived there, and that she knew what to ask for.

sections.title: The Beneficiarius
sections.body_html:
Aurelius Ammonius held a role that had no neat modern equivalent. The beneficiarius praefecti Aegypti was a soldier of special rank detached from regular military duty and assigned to the provincial governor’s staff.46 The title derived from the Latin beneficium – a favor, a privilege – and originally referred to soldiers who had received some special appointment from their commander. By the late third century, the role had crystallized into something like a provincial police officer and magistrate rolled into one, stationed at fixed posts called stationes throughout the province.47

His name tells us something. Ammonius is Egyptian – derived from Ammon, the great god of the Western Desert, whose oracle at Siwa had once drawn Alexander the Great across the sand. The nomen Aurelius, as with Techosis herself, marks him as a Roman citizen. He was, in all probability, an Egyptian who had entered Roman military service, risen to a special assignment, and been posted back to a city in the province of his birth. Whether he was from Oxyrhynchus or stationed there from elsewhere, we cannot say. But when Techosis stood before him, she was addressing a man who likely spoke her language – or at least one of them – and who understood the world she came from.

We can see what a beneficiarius did from other papyri. One document, roughly contemporary, preserves a terse order: “From the beneficiarius. Hand over Psais son of Vibius, priest of Athena, to the person despatched, or come yourselves.”48 Another shows a beneficiarius ordering village officials to deliver a prisoner: “Deliver up to my officer whom I have sent Pachoumis, son of Pachoumis, whom you have arrested today and brought to your village, being a citizen. If you have anything to say in his favour, come with him and tell me.”49 The tone in both cases is military, direct, impatient. No explanation is offered. No reason is given. The beneficiarius issues a command, and the command is expected to be obeyed.

These were men who could compel. They had soldiers they could dispatch. They had the authority of the praefect behind their orders. And they dealt not only with criminals and prisoners but with the routine disputes of civilian life – inheritance quarrels, property crimes, neighborhood violence.

A petition from 297 CE, just two years after Techosis’s, shows a woman named Aurelia Taesis, daughter of Kopres, from the village of Karanis, petitioning a beneficiarius named Aurelius Gordianos about violence she had suffered: “Having suffered violence and an unlawful attack…”50 The parallel is almost exact. Two women, in the same decade, in the same province, turning to the same kind of officer for the same kind of relief. The beneficiarius was, for women like Techosis and Taesis, the accessible face of Roman justice – not the distant praefect in Alexandria, but the officer in the local statio, the man who could actually compel their assailants to appear.

sections.title: The Law of Intestate Succession
sections.body_html:
Techosis’s legal position rested on a single phrase: kleronomo kata tous nomous – “heir in accordance with the laws.”51 Her mother had died without making a will. Under Roman intestate succession – which applied to all citizens in Egypt after the Constitutio Antoniniana of 212 CE extended Roman citizenship throughout the empire – children were the primary heirs.52 Daughters inherited alongside sons. No guardian was needed for the inheritance itself, though women might need a kyrios – a male legal representative – for certain legal transactions.53 By the late third century, this requirement had been substantially relaxed. Women with three or more children could act independently under the ius liberorum, the “right of children,” and the papyri show numerous women conducting legal business on their own. As early as 257 CE, a woman named Aurelia Sarapias had sold property in Oxyrhynchus “acting without a guardian by right of her children according to Roman custom.”83 Whether Techosis had children of her own – whether she qualified for the ius liberorum – the petition does not say. It did not need to. For the purpose of claiming an intestate inheritance, her status as daughter was sufficient.

The alternative was the state. The Gnomon of the Idios Logos, the great administrative code governing fiscal matters in Egypt, was blunt: “Of those who die intestate and have no other heir according to the laws, their property is adjudicated to the fiscus.”54 The Roman treasury stood ready to absorb any estate that no legitimate heir claimed. Techosis’s petition, by asserting her status as heir, was simultaneously a claim against Sotas and Papontos and a preemptive defense against the state’s own fiscal appetite.

Other women in Roman Egypt navigated these same waters. In 269 CE, a woman named Aurelia Tinoutis – also called Theodora – petitioned to decline an intestate brother’s estate, precisely because he had died in debt: “Whereas then my brother on my father’s side died intestate and childless, and whereas I touched nothing of his nor entered upon the inheritance…”55 Inheritance was not always a gift. It could be a trap. The Roman principle of universal succession meant that heirs inherited debts as well as assets, and a prudent woman who suspected her relative had died owing more than he owned would formally renounce the inheritance before the debts could attach to her.

Techosis’s mother, however, had left gold and fine clothing and furniture. This was an estate worth claiming. The question was not whether to inherit but how to reclaim what had already been stolen.

The legal setting also explains why Techosis emphasized her filial devotion so heavily. Roman law recognized the principle that heirs who had cared for the deceased had a stronger moral – if not always strictly legal – claim than those who had been absent or negligent.56 By stressing that she nursed her mother, paid for the funeral, and performed “what is fitting,” Techosis was not merely expressing grief. She was constructing a legal argument that anticipated challenges to her status as heir.

And her legal strategy extended beyond establishing her claim. The petition asks for two specific remedies, and the choice is revealing. She does not ask the beneficiarius to recover the property. She does not ask him to arrest Sotas and Papontos. Instead, she asks for something more procedural: that the beneficiarius compel the two men to provide written security – engrapha mones kai emphaneias – guaranteeing that they will stay in the area and present themselves when summoned.71 This is a request for a bond, not a verdict. Techosis is not asking Aurelius Ammonius to judge her case. She is asking him to ensure that the defendants cannot disappear before a higher authority can hear it.

Her second request makes this explicit: she wants the beneficiarius to forward a copy of her petition to the praefect’s office – te hegemonia – because she intends to pursue the matter there.72 The beneficiarius is a waystation, not a destination. Techosis understands – or Agathodaemon understands, or both of them understand – that the real power lies in Alexandria, in the governor’s court, where a formal hearing might compel the return of the stolen goods. The beneficiarius is the gatekeeper. The petition asks him to open the gate.

This two-step approach – secure the defendants locally, then escalate to the provincial authority – reveals a legal literacy that the illiterate petitioner acquired not from books but from the world she lived in. In Roman Egypt, everyone knew someone who had filed a petition. Everyone knew how the system was supposed to work, even if it often did not.

sections.title: A House of Many Doors
sections.body_html:
The most revealing detail in the petition is the phrase “my neighbours in the same house where my mother lived” – geitonas mou en te aute oikia en e katoikei he meter mou.57 Sotas and Papontos were not strangers who broke in from the street. They were co-residents of the same building, sharing corridors and courtyards, perhaps sharing a water supply or a staircase. The theft was not a burglary in the modern sense. It was a neighbor walking through an internal door.

Urban housing in Roman Oxyrhynchus was dense, multi-unit, and architecturally promiscuous.58 Houses were divided and subdivided over generations through inheritance, sale, and partition. A building that had once belonged to a single family might, a century later, house three or four separate households, each occupying a suite of rooms connected by shared passages. Property documents from the city describe houses in fractions – “two shares out of five shares of a two-storied house and court” – reflecting ownership patterns that made every wall a potential boundary dispute.59 You might own a room on the ground floor and share the courtyard with three other families. You might own the upper story and have the right to pass through someone else’s ground-floor corridor to reach the stairs. The boundaries were legal, not physical. A wall that separated your property from your neighbor’s looked the same from both sides.

In this architecture, privacy was partial at best. You could hear your neighbors cook, argue, cough, and die. You could smell what they were eating. You knew when they left the house and when they came back. And they knew the same about you.

In such a building, a death created vulnerability. The deceased’s rooms were suddenly unoccupied, at least temporarily. The surviving heir might be elsewhere – at the undertaker’s, at the temple, at the office of the local registrar. The neighbors knew this. They knew when the daughter left. They knew what the mother had owned. And they knew which doors were between them and the dead woman’s possessions.

We should pause on the names. Sotas is Greek, or at least Greek-sounding. Papontos is Egyptian. This mixed naming is entirely typical of late-third-century Oxyrhynchus, where the old ethnic categories – Greek, Egyptian, Roman – had blurred into practical irrelevance. What mattered was not ancestry but proximity. These two men lived close enough to the dead woman’s quarters to know what was inside them. They may have known her for years. They may have heard her coughing through the walls.

A parallel case from the sixth century captures the dynamic precisely: “The people of P… have come and informed me that certain neighbours have again attacked them and ventured to carry off… and other objects belonging to the people of the said village.”60 The word “again” is chilling. Neighborly theft was not an aberration. It was a pattern – the kind of crime that proximity enabled and that the law struggled to prevent because the thieves lived, literally, on the other side of the wall.

An earlier burglary report from Oxyrhynchus, dated to 190 CE, describes thieves who “broke down a door that led into the public street and had been blocked up with bricks, probably using a log of wood as a battering-ram. They then entered the house and contented themselves with taking from what was stored there 10 artabae of barley.”76 In that case, at least, the thieves came from outside. Sotas and Papontos did not need a battering ram. They were already inside.

Techosis says she does not know on what grounds they acted – “on what inducement I cannot tell” – but the inducement seems plain enough. They saw an opportunity. A woman alone, a woman from elsewhere, occupied with death, in a building they knew intimately. The gold and the clothing and the furniture were right there, behind a door they could open. The phrase “I cannot tell” may be genuine bewilderment. It may also be legal strategy – a way of denying, in advance, any justification the thieves might offer. By professing ignorance of their motives, Techosis forecloses the possibility that they had a legitimate claim.

sections.title: The Illiterate Petitioner
sections.body_html:
“Written for her by Aurelius Agathodaemon son of Serenus, as she stated that she did not know letters.”61

This sentence, a standard closing formula, tells us something important about Techosis and something important about Roman Egypt. She could not read or write. She stated this openly, without apparent shame, as a matter of fact. And she obtained justice – or at least access to justice – anyway.

Illiteracy in late Roman Egypt was not the barrier to legal participation that it might seem. The apparatus of professional scribes, petition-writers, and legal advocates meant that a person who could not sign her own name could nonetheless file a sophisticated legal document invoking Roman inheritance law, requesting specific remedies, and announcing her intention to appeal to the praefect.62 The scribe was not a luxury. He was infrastructure – as essential to the functioning of the legal system as the beneficiarius himself. Without men like Agathodaemon, the petitions of the illiterate would never have been written, and the complaints of the poor would never have reached the officials who could act on them. The Roman state generated paper in quantities that would not be matched in Europe for a thousand years. The scribes were the ones who fed the machine.

Agathodaemon’s fee does not survive. Scribal fees are rarely recorded in the petitions themselves, though they appear in other kinds of documents. He may have been a professional scribe, sitting in or near the beneficiarius’s statio, available to anyone who walked in with a grievance and a few coins. He may have been a friend or a relative of Techosis, performing a favor. He may have been an acquaintance from a Small Oasis community in Oxyrhynchus, if migrants from the oasis clustered together as migrants often do. The petition names him, thanks him for his literacy, and tells us nothing else.

The phenomenon is visible across the papyrological record. “I, Aurelius Tima[genes?] wrote for her as she is illiterate,” reads one document from 245 CE.63 “I, Aurelius Origenes, wrote on behalf of my mother, who is illiterate,” says another from 257 CE.64 A woman in Karanis in 299 CE – the same decade as Techosis – had Aurelius Horion write her census declaration “since she is illiterate.”65 The formula varies slightly but the structure is constant: the petitioner speaks, the scribe writes, and the document is legally valid.

The conversation that preceded the writing is lost. Did Techosis tell Agathodaemon the story in Egyptian? In Greek? Did he ask her questions, clarify details, suggest the opening flourish about “no small danger”? Did she dictate word by word, or did she describe what had happened and leave the formal language to him? The petition is a collaboration between a woman with a grievance and a man with a pen, and the seam between their contributions is invisible.

But the grievance is hers. The anger is hers. The specificity – the gold, the furniture, the clothing, the neighbors’ names, the mother’s death, the funeral she paid for – all of this comes from a woman who knew exactly what had been taken and who had taken it. Agathodaemon may have supplied the legal Latin and the rhetorical conventions. The facts, and the fury behind them, belonged to Aurelia Techosis.

There is something else worth noting about the scribe. His name – Aurelius Agathodaemon son of Serenus – carries the nomen Aurelius, the universal mark of Roman citizenship conferred by the Constitutio Antoniniana. So does Techosis’s own name: she is Aurelia Techosis. In the Egypt of 295 CE, virtually everyone was an Aurelius or an Aurelia, the name having become less a marker of status than a kind of legal wallpaper, present on every document, signifying nothing except that the bureaucratic fiction of universal citizenship was being maintained. But the fiction mattered. It was because Techosis was a Roman citizen – however nominal that citizenship might have been for an illiterate woman from a desert oasis – that she could invoke Roman inheritance law, petition a Roman military officer, and demand that her case be forwarded to the Roman governor. The name that meant nothing also meant everything.

sections.title: "Tasabes to the Beneficiarius Against Her Children"
sections.body_html:
Turn the papyrus over. On the verso – the back of the sheet – someone has scrawled a filing note: “Tasabes to the beneficiarius against her children.”66

It is only a filing note, but it tells us what sort of desk Techosis had approached. Another woman, Tasabes, had her own complaint against her children.67 Below it comes a second scrap of docketing, “To Polydeukes from the […] before this year’s time.”68 Aurelius Ammonius was handling more than one family dispute at once. His station gathered these cases, labeled them, and either moved them onward or let them die there. Other papyri show beneficiarii doing just that: receiving complaints, ordering appearances, and forwarding matters up the chain.6970 Techosis needed her petition to be sharp enough that it would not vanish into the pile.

sections.title: What the Record Does Not Say
sections.body_html:
Techosis’s petition asks, at its close, for two specific things. First, she wants the beneficiarius to compel Sotas and Papontos to provide engrapha mones kai emphaneias – “written security of staying and appearing” – a kind of bond guaranteeing that the accused would not flee with the stolen goods before the case could be heard.71 Second, she wants the beneficiarius to forward a copy of her petition to the praefect’s office in Alexandria, because she intends to pursue the matter at the highest available level.72

The requests are precise and realistic. Techosis is not demanding immediate arrest or summary punishment. She is asking for the bureaucratic prerequisites of a formal legal proceeding: make sure the defendants cannot run, and send the paperwork to the people who can actually decide the case. This is a woman, or a scribe, or both, who understands how the system works.

An oath sworn in Oxyrhynchus a decade before Techosis’s petition shows what “appearing before the praefect” looked like in practice: “I swear by the fortune of our lord Gaius Valerius Diocletianus Caesar Augustus that I will present myself before our most eminent praefect, Marcus Aurelius Diogenes, when he auspiciously visits this place or the neighbouring nome.”79 The praefect did not sit in Alexandria waiting for petitioners to come to him. He traveled. He made circuits. He “visited” the nomes. Techosis’s plan – to escalate her case to the praefect’s office – may have meant waiting for the governor to pass through the Oxyrhynchite nome on one of these tours, or sending the petition ahead through the bureaucratic chain, or both.

Did she succeed?

The papyrus does not say. It cannot say. It is a single document, a petition filed on a single day, and no response, no verdict, no receipt of returned property survives alongside it. The papyrus was found – like almost all the Oxyrhynchus papyri – in the city’s ancient rubbish heaps, the great mounds of discarded paper that accumulated over centuries in the desert beyond the town walls.73 It was garbage. Someone threw it away.

Perhaps the beneficiarius issued the order and Sotas and Papontos returned the gold and the furniture and the fine clothes. Perhaps Techosis recovered everything and lived comfortably on her inheritance for decades. Perhaps the case was forwarded to the praefect and adjudicated in her favor. This is the optimistic reading, and there is nothing in the document to contradict it.

But there are other possibilities. Perhaps the beneficiarius did nothing. Perhaps Sotas and Papontos had connections he did not want to cross. Perhaps the petition was filed and forgotten, one more piece of paper on a desk already buried in paper. Perhaps Techosis waited for a response that never came, and eventually gave up, or died, or returned to the Small Oasis with nothing.

A darker possibility also remains: Sotas and Papontos may have claimed the property was theirs. They may have argued that the mother had given it to them, or owed it to them, or that Techosis was not the rightful heir. Neighbors who share a house with an elderly woman for years may come to feel that proximity creates entitlement. They may have helped the mother while Techosis was away. They may have lent her money, or stored her things, or simply watched her possessions with the close attention of people who live on the other side of a thin wall. The petition anticipates none of this – it presents Techosis’s version of events as simple and self-evident – but disputes over the property of the dead are rarely simple, and the neighbors’ “inducement” that Techosis claims not to understand may have been a claim of their own, one the petition strategically omits.

We hear only Techosis’s voice. Sotas and Papontos are silent – not because they had nothing to say, but because their side of the story was never written down, or was written down and lost, or was spoken in a hearing that left no record. Every petition is a partial truth. This one is partial in precisely the way its author intended.

There is also the question of what happened to Techosis herself, afterward. Did she stay in Oxyrhynchus? Did she grow old in the same building where her mother had died, passing Sotas and Papontos – or their families – in the corridor? Did she return to the Small Oasis, back across the desert to the place she came from, carrying whatever she managed to recover? Did she marry? Did she have children? Did those children nurse her when she fell ill, as she had nursed her mother?

The petition is a single point of light in a darkness that extends in every direction, and the moment we step beyond its edges we are guessing. That is the condition of ancient microhistory: the evidence is brilliant but radically incomplete, like a single tile from a mosaic that resists any full reconstruction.

The silence is the point. The papyrus preserves a voice – one voice, one moment, one woman standing in a Roman military office dictating her complaint – and then stops. The rest of the story, if there was one, has been eaten by time. What survives is the petition itself: its anger, its precision, its legal sophistication, and its portrait of a world in which an illiterate woman from a desert oasis could walk into the office of a Roman soldier and demand that the law protect her.

The petition gives us one day. Techosis buried her mother, found the rooms stripped, went to the station of a Roman officer, and asked for two practical remedies: bind Sotas and Papontos so they could not run, and send the case on to the praefect.7172 After that the record stops. No order survives. No verdict survives. Someone threw the sheet away, and the dump outside Oxyrhynchus kept it.7374

What remains is Techosis herself: a woman from the Small Oasis, living in Oxyrhynchus, unable to write and entirely able to use the law. She knew what had been taken. She knew what office could be made to hear her. She knew what she wanted done. The scribe wrote for her, but the urgency is hers. Across the broken papyrus the request is still plain: my mother’s property was taken, and I want it back.

notes.text: On the physical layout of multi-unit housing in Oxyrhynchus, see property declarations such as P.Oxy. 3.481 (corpus ID 43467), 27 August 99 CE, describing "a house and yard and other fixtures and entrances and exits," and P.Ryl. 2.160D (corpus ID 47949), 10 July 42 CE, recording the sale of "two shares out of five shares of a two-storied house and court." Such fractional ownership was the norm, not the exception, in urban Egypt.

notes.text: The phrase *kindynos ou mikros* ("no small danger") and similar rhetorical openings invoking legal consequences appear in numerous petitions. The convention served both to flatter the official's authority and to signal the petitioner's awareness of the law. See A. Z. Bryen, *Violence in Roman Egypt: A Study in Legal Interpretation* (Philadelphia, 2013) on the rhetoric of Egyptian petitions.

notes.text: On the requirement of a *kyrios* (guardian) for women's legal transactions and its gradual relaxation, see P.Oxy. 10.1277 (corpus ID 43203), 9 August 257 CE, showing a woman "acting without a guardian by right of her children according to Roman custom" (*ius liberorum*). By the late third century, the requirement was increasingly nominal.

notes.text: On the moral and legal weight of filial care in inheritance claims, see Leao, "Solon and the Obligation to Support Parents." The principle that those who cared for the deceased deserved to inherit was deeply embedded in both Greek and Egyptian tradition, and was reinforced by Roman legal practice.

notes.text: P.Oxy. 8.1118 (corpus ID 44136), 1st-2nd century CE: a creditor in the Nile valley petitioning to have the strategus of the Small Oasis compel a debtor to pay "the debt and the interest due in addition and extras, or else that they may know that I shall enter on the mortgaged property." notes.text: P.Oxy. 9.1210 (corpus ID 42653), 1st century BCE -- 1st century CE: a tax register listing "Men chosen by the parents from their sons to support them in old age, in the Oxyrhynchite nome." The register demonstrates that *geroboskia* -- care for the elderly -- was not only a moral obligation but an administratively recognized category with implications for taxation and civic duty.

notes.text: P.Sakaon 41 (corpus ID 24816), 14 July 323 CE: a petition from a bereaved man in the Fayum whose wife and children had died, after which local tax collectors began attacking him. The *ius liberorum* was established under Augustan marriage legislation and granted legal independence to women who had borne three or more children (four for freedwomen).

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 8
              In the consulship of the present consuls. To Aurelius Ammonius, beneficiarius of the praefect of Egypt, from Aurelia Techosis daughter of Diodorus and Techosis, of the Small Oasis, now living at the illustrious and most illustrious city of Oxyrhynchus. No small danger and no ordinary severity awaits those who lightly give themselves over to plunder and robbery of the property of others. I therefore, being the victim of a most outrageous attack and robbery, approach you to testify to the assault upon me. Techosis, my aforesaid mother, was stricken with illness, and I in the goodness of my heart nursed and tended her and was assiduous in performing what is owing from children to parents. When a few days ago she died intestate, leaving me her daughter heir in accordance with the law, I provided for her funeral and did all that was fitting on the occasion of her death. While I was occupied with my trouble, I know not on what ground or with what impulse, a certain Sotas and Papontos, who are my neighbours in the same house where my mother lived, possessing themselves of the extensive movables left by her, a considerable amount of gold, a quantity of furniture, some very valuable clothes, and other things, lawlessly carried them all off, on what inducement I cannot tell. I am about to demand satisfaction for this of the superior official, and in order that they may put in an appearance I perforce present this petition, testifying to the assault and requesting that they may be compelled to provide written security that they will stay and appear, since I am already having recourse to the official, and that a copy of this document be prepared through your grace for the praefect’s office. Date and signature of Aurelia Techosis, written for her by Aurelius Agathodaemon.

sources.description: Beneficiarius ordering delivery of prisoner Pachoumis

sources.greek: π αρὰ τοῦ στατίζοντος β ενε φ ικιαρίου κωμάρχαις κώμης Τερύθεως. παράδοτε τῷ ἀποσταλέντι ὑπηρέτ κωμάρχαις κώμης Τερύθεως. παράδοτε τῷ ἀποσταλέντι ὑπηρέτ ῃ Παχοῦμιν Παχοῦμιν Παχούμιος Παχοῦμις ὃν κατεσχήκατε σήμερον καὶ κατηνέγ κατε ἐν τῇ κώμῃ ὑμῶν πολίτην ὄντα. εἰ δὲ ἔχετε εὐλογίαν τινὰ πρὸς αὐτὸν ὑμῶν πολίτην ὄντα. εἰ δὲ ἔχετε εὐλογίαν τινὰ πρὸς αὐτὸν ἀνέρχεσθε ἅμα αὐτῷ καὶ λέγετε. ἀλλʼ ὅρα μὴ κατάσχητε τὸν ἀνέρχεσθε ἅμα αὐτῷ καὶ λέγετε. ἀλλʼ ὅρα μὴ κατάσχητε τὸν ὑπη ρέτη ρέτη ν ὑπη ρετη ρετη . σεση μείωμαι .
sources.translation: Translation: from P.Oxy. 1 From the beneficiarius on duty to the comarchs of the village of Teruthis. Deliver up to my officer whom I have sent Pachoumis, son of Pachoumis, whom you have arrested today and brought to your village, being a citizen. If you have anything to say in his favour, come with him and tell me. See that you do not detain the officer. Signed.

sources.greek: π αρὰ τοῦ β ενε φ ικιαρίου . παράδοτε τῷ ἀποσταλέντι Ψά παράδοτε τῷ ἀποσταλέντι Ψά ι ν Οὐ ί βιος ἱερέα Ἀθηνᾶ ς ἢ ἢ ὑ μεῖς ἥκετε. κωμάρχαις καὶ ἐπιστάτῃ εἰρήνης Ἀράβων. κωμάρχαις καὶ ἐπιστάτῃ εἰρήνης Ἀράβων.
sources.translation:
From the beneficiarius. Hand over Psais son of Vibius, priest of Athena, to the person despatched,
or come yourselves.
To the comarchs and the epistates eirenes of Arabon.  (D. Colomo)

sources.translation: In the consulship of our lords the Flavii Valentinianus and Valens and Gratianus, the eternal Augusti, for the 2nd time. Aurelius Athenodoros, public physician, from Hermoupolis the most brilliant, making the case on behalf of Sarapiaine daughter of Paxamos [...] and metropolite, by commission also received in the prefectural records, to the Flavii Herakleon, beneficiarius, and Isido[...] and Kyra, heirs of Nemesilla, from the same city, adversaries, greetings. I, Athenodoros, having been permitted, have summoned you many times, but you, avoiding the direct course, have resorted to pretexts. But now too the case has come before me without authority, since my lord the most illustrious prefect Flavius Herakleios has decreed that there should be [...] annulment of the renewal which I obtained, once the time limits have expired. Wherefore now too, having received authority, I summon you in accordance with the judgment, which reads as follows [...].

sources.translation: Valerius Pompeianus to the strategi of the epistrategy of the Heptanomia and the Small Oasis. Concerning the confiscation of the property of hypomnematographoi and [...], through Theodoros, protector. Valerius Pompeianus to the strategi of the epistrategy of the Heptanomia and the Small Oasis, greetings. Certain of the hypomnematographoi and [...] by the late Sallustius of most distinguished memory, having made a careful assessment, report to me with the utmost speed. [...] I shall penalize you [...] having been ordered [...] year 5 and year 4, Mesore 6. I happen from the letters written at other times concerning [...] Ianos and Apollonios, sons of Neilos also called Posidonios [...] 30 talents. I have sent down through the Aurelii Auxon and Demetrios [...] to be delivered in Alexandria to the city account [...] of the monies, necessarily, of the two months [...] the 30 talents of silver brought down, having appended [...]. Year 5 and year 4, Hathyr. Valerius Pompeianus to the strategi of the Heptanomia and the Thebaid, concerning the dispatch of Paulinos. Valerius Pompeianus to the strategi of the Heptanomia and the Thebaid, greetings. Appoint therefore to him the [...].

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 6
              Proclamation of his excellency Flavius Valerius Pompeianus, praefect of Egypt. Orphans for whom no guardians have been assigned shall have guardians in accordance with their age created for them by those competent to make the appointment ...; for it will thus result that they receive proper attention, whereas at present much business concerning orphans and depending upon their guardians is delayed because the orphans are unattended by tutores or curatores. The 4th which = the 3rd year of our lords the Augusti Diocletianus and Maximianus, Phaophi... Published in Oxyrhynchus on the 247th of the same month Phaophi.
              To ..., exegetes in office of the Oxyrhynchite nome and the Small Oasis, senator of the illustrious and most illustrious city of Oxyrhynchus, from ... and Apollonia, both children of Origenes and Thaësis, of the illustrious and most illustrious city of Oxyrhynchus. We were given the guardianship of our nephews (or nieces), the children of our dead sister
              ... daughter of Origenes, of the said city...

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 8
              ... I beg you to write to the strategus of the Small Oasis to present to ... and Chenenoubis, through the one of them who may be found, a copy of this memorandum, in order that he may yet pay to me the debt and the interest due in addition and extras, or else that they may know that I shall enter on the mortgaged property and shall occupy and exercise ownership over it and alienate it and do with it whatever I choose, without any claim being left to them or to any one else for the future in any respect ...

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 9
              In the consulships of our lords the Emperors Diocletianus Augustus for the seventh and Maximianus Augustus for the sixth time. To Aurelius Zenogenes, strategus of the Oxyrhynchite nome, from Aurelius Plutarchus also called Atactius, excellency, and however I am styled. Having been nominated wrongfully and in contravention of all law for the decemprimate by Aurelius Demetrianus, decemprimus of the western toparchy, I brought an action of appeal through my father Aurelius Sarapammon also called Dionysius, and however he is styled, because I was at the time in the Small Oasis for the discharge of the soldiers stationed there, in accordance with the order of my lord the most honourable praefect of Egypt Aelius Publius; and having taken the proper steps for the appeal I had recourse to my lord the most honourable catholicus Pomponius Domnus, and applied to him in a memorandum setting these facts before him. Whereas then his highness ordered me by a judgement to give notice to the aforesaid person, the essential part of the proceedings and the judgement being as follows :—
              In the consulship of our lords Diocletianus Augustus for the seventh time and Maximianus Augustus for the sixth time, August 19, at Alexandria, in court. Plutarchus, excellency, having been summoned, Isidorus said, “His excellency Plutarchus who has presented himself before your eminence, endeavouring to find for himselfa release from municipal offices, some time ago besought the divine fortune of our masters the Augusti and Caesars to grant him the rank of excellency, and their divine fortune consented and granted it,and he now enjoys it. Now he has continued in obedience to your lordship’s department and also to the orders of you magnates. Lately when he was in the Small Oasis, where he had been sent by my lord your colleague Publius the most honourable praefect to discharge the soldiers, a certain Demetrianus, an Oxyrhynchite of the same city, made a design upon him and ventured to nominate him for the decemprimate, ignoring his acquisition of a superior rank, which presumably releases him from municipal offices”. After other evidence Domnus the most honourable catholicus said, “Read the day of his appointment”. Plutarchus said, “Pauni 30. I was away in the Oasis; I came back when I knew”. Domnus the most honourable catholicus said, “Let the document containing the appointment be produced, and let him also show the following correspondence, as I ordered; and
              that he may be heard in a more regular way, let him give notice to the person who nominated (?) him for the decemprimate”. Gregorius said, “Give orders for the issue of the minutes”. Domnus the most honourable catholicus said, “They shall be issued”. I, Olympius, official notary, issued the minutes.
              Wherefore I beg, if it seem good to you, ...

Αὐρ ήλιος Παποντὼς ὑπηρέτης βουλευτικὸς ἐπήνεγκα. ἔτους η καὶ ζ , Μεχεὶρ ι ϛ .
sources.translation: Translation: from P.Oxy. 1 After an elaborate introduction, in which the council of Oxyrhynchus through its president Aurelius Apollo addresses the strategus Aurelius Apollonius through Asclepiades, the papyrus proceeds (line 8):—At a meeting of our body a dispatch was read from Theodorus who was recently chosen in place of Arion the scribe to proceed to his highness the praefect and attend his immaculate court. In this dispatch he explained that he is a victor in the games and exempt from inquiries ... We have therefore nominated Aurelius ... to serve, and we send you word accordingly, in order that this fact may be brought to his knowledge, and no time be lost in his departure and attendance upon the court. We pray for your health, dearest brother.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 10
              To Claudius Dioscurides also called Chaereas, ex-strategus of the Diopolite nome, strategus of the Oxyrhynchite nome, from Aurelius Papontos son of Theon and Aurelius Horus son of Archelaus, both comarchs of the village of Ision Panga for the present 8th which = the 7th year. Having been enjoined by you to keep in safety the crops at the threshing-floors in our lands until the decaproti have received payment in full of the public taxes from each person, we accordingly agree, swearing by the fortune of our lords Diocletian and Maximian Augusti, to be on the watch and to permit no one to touch the produce until each person has paid to the local decaproti the amount due from him, the measurement being made so that no complaint may ensue; otherwise may we be liable to the penalties attaching to the oath. The 8th which is also the 7th year of our lords Diocletian and Maximian Augusti, Pauni 19.

sources.translation:
To the board of laographoi through one of them, Aurelius Diogenes alias Hermias, of the splendid
and most splendid city of the Oxyrhynchites, for the current 8th year and 7th year, from Aurelius
(?)Horion son of Theon, mother . . . , from the splendid and most splendid city of the Oxyrhynchites.
I wish to register with you for the first time the son of my deceased friend —ion son of Sarapion, —es,
of mother Adora, in the quarter of . . . , who in the current 8th year and 7th year is 12 years old (of the
twelve-drachma gymnasial class?). Therefore I submit the application requesting that he be enrolled
in the category of his age group as is appropriate, and I swear the oath customary to Romans not to
have lied. Year 8 of Imperator Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus and year 7 of Imperator
Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Germanici Maximi Pii Felices Augusti, Mesore 19.
(2nd hand) . . . 12 years old . . . son of the great(?) . . . Mermertha . . .  (N. Gonis)

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 6
              In the consulship of Valerius Constantius and Valerius Maximianus, the most renowned Caesars, The most high senate of the illustrious and most illustrious city of Oxyrhynchus through Aurelius Cornelianus ..., prytanis in office, to their dearest Ptoleminus also called Sarmates, exegetes, greeting. An exegetes being required for the ... of Epeiph up to the 17th, it was decided that you should preside, while the expenses should be paid by the whole body of those belonging to the order. This letter is accordingly sent to you, dear friend, for your information, We pray for your health, dear friend.

sources.translation: Translation: from P.Oxy. 1 To his excellency Clodius Culcianus, praefect of Egypt, from Aurelius Demetrius, son of Nilus, late chief priest at Arsinoe. Knowing your care for honest citizens, my lord praefect, I make my petition to you with full confidence that I shall obtain justice from your highness. In the 17th = the 16th = the 9th year of this auspicious reign Aurelius Sotas, ex-gymnasiarch of Arsinoe, acknowledged in two bonds the receipt of a fully secured deposit from me, the first bond, which was made in the month of Tybi, being for two talents of silver; the second, which was made in Phamenoth, for twenty talents of silver. These sums he, by the terms of the aforesaid contracts, undertook to repay without an action at law or any delay or quibble. When therefore I asked him for the money while Heron was strategus, he attempted, owing to my being illiterate, to commit a fraud to my detriment. When he was detected in this and was in danger of being prosecuted before your highness, he entreated to be allowed to settle his debts without the trouble of an action. Up to the present moment he is still putting off the payment, taking a mean advantage of my forbearance, while I am in debt to the most sacred treasury not only on account of the deficit in connexion with the duty which I have performed as superintendent of the corn-supply, but also in connexion with both my private estate and the municipal post which I undertook, and I have no other resources than this money in question. On all these counts therefore I beg and entreat you to instruct, if you will, the strategus or any other magistrate whom you may sanction, that Sotas shall be compelled by seizure of the securities provided in his written bonds now at length to make repayment, or that, if he is recalcitrant, he shall be summoned before your highness to answer for his previous fraud also. So I shall be enabled to recover my property and acknowledge my gratitude to your excellency. Farewell. I, Aurelius Demetrius presented this petition. The 19th = 18th year, Phamenoth 4.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 8
              The sixth consulship of our lords the Emperors Constantius and Maximianus. To his dearest Aurelius Seuthis, also called Horion, logistes of the Oxyrhynchite nome, from Aurelius Hieracion also called Noninus, ex-gymnasiarch, ex-prytanis, senator, prytanis in office of the illustrious and most illustrious city of Oxyrhynchus, greeting. I request that orders may now be given to pay me out of the city’s funds from the sums publicly apportioned ... to be used for the expenses in accordance with the letters of his highness my lord the praefect Clodius Culcianus which I lately handed to you ... a further sum of fifty talents and four hundred and fifty denarii of silver, total 50 tal. 450 den. I think it right that the curator of the civic chest should be sent to make this payment to me, so that there may be no obstruction to the interests of the public baths or to the other interests of my prytany. I pray for your health, dearest friend. Date by the regnal years of the emperors, and declaration of the curator (?) that he had paid over the money and obtained a receipt.

sources.translation:
(From: J. Rowlandson, Women and Society in Greek and Roman Egypt, 1998, no. 140b.)

Latin To Valerius Firmus, prefect of Egypt, from Aurelia Arsinoe. I ask you, my lord, [to give me as a guardian according to the lex Iulia et Titia and the decree of the senate Aurelius] Herminos. Year 2, Pachon 26. Sheet 94, vol. 1 .
Greek Translation of the Latin: 
To Valerius Firmus, prefect of Egypt, from [Aurelia Arsinoe. I ask you, my lord, to give me]  as a guardian according to the lex Iulia et [Titia] [Aureius Herminos. Presented] on the 12th day before the Kalends of June in the consulship of the Emperor Philippus Augustus and Titianus. I, Aurelia, Arsinoe daughter of Sarapion, [have presented this petition, requesting that Aurelius Hermi]nos should be appointed my guardian. I, Aurelius Tima[genes ?, son of … wrote for her as she is illiterate.  I, Aurelius Herminos son of Dionysios, consent to the petition. Year 2, Pachon 26.]
Unless you have the right to another guardian, [I grant you the guardian] for whom [you ask].

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 10
              Aurelia Sarapias daughter of Arius, citizen, acting without a guardian by right of her children according to Roman custom, to Aurelius Theon son of Ammonius also called Aphunchis,of Oxyrhynchus, greeting. I acknowledge that I have sold to you a three-sided couch with linen coverings embroidered throughout, and four ... linen cushions of the same quality for the price of five hundred drachmae, total 500 dr., which I thereupon received. This contract of sale of which there is a single copy, free from mistake, is valid, and I will guarantee the sale and have been asked the formal question, as aforesaid. The and year of the emperors and Caesars Publius Licinius Valerianus and Publius Licinius Valerianus Gallienus Germanici Maximi and Publius Licinius Cornelius Valerianus the most illustrious Caesar Augusti, Mesore 16. (Signed) I, Aurelia Sarapias, have sold the couch and cushions and received the 500 dr. for the price and will guarantee the sale, and have been asked the formal question, as aforesaid. I, Aurelius Origenes, wrote on behalf of my mother, who is illiterate.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 8
              Concerning the outrage suffered at his abode by my son-in-law Polydeuces I presented to the officials a petition against the perpetrator, Eudaemon; but his influence procured the failure of the petition, so that he should not seem indictable. I accordingly testify to his violence, being a feeble widow woman. For Thonis the curator of Seuthes rushed into my house and dared to carry off my slave Theodora, though he had no power over her, so that I am subjected to unmitigated violence.

sources.translation:
‘Aurelius Tyrannius also called Ammonius, strategus of the Hermopolite nome, to Hermes assistant … A copy is forwarded to you of the petition of Aurelia Tinoutis also called Theodora daughter of Pinoution in order that you make it known to the person therein stated. The 1st year of our lord Claudius Caesar Augustus, Pachon 26. Signed by me.’
‘To Aurelius Tyrannius also called Ammonius, strategus of the Hermopolite nome, from Aurelia Tinoutis also called Theodora, daughter of Pinoution son of Morus also called …, of Hermopolis, registered in the quarter of the Western Guard-house, with the concurrence of my husband Aurelius Nilammon son of Pinoution son of ..., his mother being Thaesis, of the same city, the subscription being written for us by Aurelius Copreas also called Agathodemon, of the same city, registered in the East-end quarter, since we are illiterate. As you are aware, it is clearly stated in the Imperial laws that those who have inherited nothing from deceased persons cannot be held responsible for their debts or the claims made against them. Whereas then my brother on my father's side Aurelius Copreas, with whom I have no partnership and who lived alone …, died intestate and childless, and whereas I touched nothing of his nor entered upon the inheritance, and whereas Aurelius Eudaemon, cosmetes, son of Eudaemon ex-agonothetes and ex-senator of the same city of Hermopolis, professing to be a creditor of his persecutes and annoys me unjustly, and I am ignorant whether there are other creditors of my said brother, I therefore am compelled to present this petition to you, renouncing and resigning to Eudaemon the inheritance of my said brother in compensation for the sums due to him and for any others which may be claimed on my said brother's account, and I request you to instruct an assistant to present a duplicate of this petition to him for his information, so that I may keep my property in freedom, inviolate, and undisturbed in respect of my brother, of whose property I possess nothing, according to the Imperial ordinances. Farewell. 
The 1st year of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Pius Felix Augustus, Pachon 17.’ Signatures of Aurelia Tinoutis and her husband, written by Copreas, and of Aurelius Eudaemon acknowledging receipt of the document.

sources.translation: To Claudius Alexandros, centurion, from Panetbeous, public farmer from the village of Theadelphia. My cohabiting wife, from whom I also had children, having conceived hostile intentions toward our life together, took advantage of my absence and left my house some months ago without the so-called formal divorce, having carried off both her own things and very many of ours, among which was a large white undressed garment, an Oxyrhynchite pillow, a cross-striped double-blanket, materials for two tunics, and [...] and other agricultural tools. And although I sent to her many times, wishing to recover our property, she, being unrepentant, returned nothing -- despite the fact that I provided her with the maintenance allowance for the child. Since then I have now learned that a certain Neilos son of Syros from the same village has unlawfully seized and married her, I submit this petition and request that she and Neilos be brought before you, so that I may be able to obtain justice and recover our property, and I shall have been assisted. Farewell.

sources.translation: Translation: from P.Oxy. 1 ...they broke down a door that led into the public street and had been blocked up with bricks, probably using a log of wood as a battering-ram. They then entered the house and contented themselves with taking from what was stored there 10 artabae of barley, which they carried off by the same way. We guessed that this was removed piecemeal by the said door from the marks of a rope dragged along in that direction, and pointed out this fact to the chief of the police of the village and to the other officials. I am therefore obliged to put in this petition, and beg you to order that the chief of the police and the other officials be brought before you, and to make due inquiry about the robbery, so that I may be able to recover the barley.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 8
              Kommon son of Taur... to Paulus son of (?) Sillagr... brother of Narroous, Pachon 23. The people of P... have come and informed me that certain neighbours have again attacked them and ventured to [carry off ...] and other objects belonging to the people of the said village. Go to the said village and preserve their inviolability, and if any persons attempt to do any lawless action to them or even to contemplate it, instruct their leaders to abstain from any such outrage. For if they persist, a troop of soldiers will come on them and seize and hand them over to a tribunal to be punished for the lawlessness upon which they may venture. (Subscribed) Read by me. Read by me, the scribe. Address on the verso.
